data.title: Abacus Global Management announces $20M share repurchase program
data.description:
• Abacus Global Management (ABX) announced on Friday that its Board of Directors has authorized a $20M share repurchase program, effective January 30, 2026.
  • Abacus expects to fund the share repurchase program through cash on hand and free cash flow. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: OverActive Media announces private placement extension
data.description:
• OverActive Media (OAM:CA) (OTC: OAMCF) said on Friday it has extended its previously announced non-brokered private placement of up to 10 million units priced at $0.30 each, for gross proceeds of up to about $3 million.
  • The company said further closings of the offering are now expected to occur by February 23, 2026, following an initial closing on December 30, 2025, subject to regulatory approval and customary conditions.
  • Each unit consists of one common share and one warrant, with each warrant exercisable at $0.40 for 24 months, subject to acceleration.
  • OAM:CA closed -0.0% at $0.245.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: AZZ Inc. authorizes new $100M share repurchase program
data.description:
• AZZ (AZZ) on Friday said its board approved a new share repurchase program authorizing the company to buy back up to $100 million of its common stock, effective immediately.
  • The new program will run alongside a prior $100 million repurchase authorization approved in 2020, under which about $33.2 million remained available as of November 30, 2025.
  • The company had about 30.0 million shares outstanding at the end of its third quarter of fiscal 2026.
  • AZZ said repurchases may be made in the open market or through private transactions, including under Rule 10b5-1 trading plans.
  • AZZ -0.0% after hours to $124.29
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Granada Gold Mine announces $2.5M private placement
data.description:
• Granada Gold Mine (GGM:CA) announced on Friday a non-brokered private placement offering raising gross proceeds of  up to $2.5M through the issuance of up to 50,000,000 units at $0.05 per unit. 
  • Each unit is comprised of one common share of the company and one common share purchase warrant of the company. Each warrant entitles the holder thereof to purchase one common share at a price of $0.075 per common share for a period of five years from the date of issuance.
  • The company may pay finders' fees in connection with the offering to eligible arm's-length finders.
  •  The company intends to use net proceeds of the offering for a resource update, exploration, and general corporate purposes for the company's Granada Gold Property, near Rouyn-Noranda, Quebec.
  •  Source: Press Release

data.title: Flagstar forecasts 2026 EPS of $0.65-$0.70 as bank returns to profitability and accelerates C&I growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flagstar Bank (FLG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Otting, President, CEO & Executive Chairman, stated that the bank returned to profitability in the fourth quarter, reporting adjusted net income of $30 million or $0.06 per diluted share, compared to a net loss of $0.07 per diluted share in the previous quarter. Otting highlighted "growth in our net interest income, coupled with NIM expansion and disciplined expense management" as key drivers of performance and noted a $45 million increase in pre-provision net revenue and approximately 900 basis points of positive operating leverage.
  • Otting reported continued C&I loan growth—"up a 2% on a linked quarter basis or about 9% on an annualized basis"—and a reduction of $2.3 billion in multifamily and CRE loans, with the CRE concentration ratio now below 400%. Credit quality improved as nonaccrual loans declined, and there were decreases in net charge-offs and the provision for loan losses.
  • Otting stated, "We expect that in 2026, our earning power will continue to strengthen with a full year of profitability driven by continued growth in net interest income and margin expansion, along with a continued focus on managing our expenses lower."
  • Lee Smith, Senior Executive VP & CFO, remarked, "We're obviously very pleased with our performance in the fourth quarter and for the full year in 2025. We're executing on our strategic vision and have returned the bank to profitability as we said we would do." Smith highlighted a 14 basis points NIM expansion quarter-over-quarter, after adjusting for a one-time hedge gain, and reported paying off $1.7 billion in brokered deposits and $1 billion in club advances, reducing funding costs. Smith also noted a reduction in nonaccruals of $267 million, a $26 million decline in net charge-offs, and a $35 million decrease in provision for loan losses.

Outlook

  • The company reported updated EPS guidance for 2026 in the range of $0.65 to $0.70 and for 2027 in the range of $1.90 to $2. Otting stated, "We remain focused on further improving the bank's credit profile as we proactively manage our CRE exposure lower through par payoffs and opportunistic loan sales, reducing nonaccruals and a lower level of charge-offs."
  • Smith noted, "We slightly adjusted our net interest income guidance for both '26 and '27 as a result of higher payoffs and a smaller balance sheet."
  • Guidance language pointed to balance sheet growth in 2026 and positive operating leverage, with a continued focus on expense management and C&I lending expansion.

Financial Results

  • The bank reported net income attributable to common stockholders of $0.05 per diluted share and adjusted net income of $0.06 per diluted share for the fourth quarter.
  • Smith reported, "Our unadjusted pre-provision pretax net revenue improved $51 million quarter-over-quarter, while our adjusted pre-provision pretax net revenue improved $45 million versus Q3."
  • Net interest margin expanded 23 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 2.14% with a one-time $20 million hedge gain; excluding this, NIM was 2.05%, a 14 basis point increase.
  • Core operating expenses declined approximately $700 million comparing full year 2025 to 2024. The CET1 capital ratio increased to 12.83%. The bank reduced brokered deposits by $1.7 billion and FHLB advances by $1 billion in the quarter. Overall CRE balances declined $12.1 billion since year-end 2023.

Q&A

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Asked about the $100 million reduction in net interest income guidance. Smith responded, "It's the higher payoff activity, particularly as it relates to multifamily and CRE loans... as I mentioned, we paid down $1 billion of flow, $1.7 billion of brokered deposits."
  • Dave Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about funding loan growth with core deposits. Smith replied, "We feel pretty good. As we think about core deposit growth, I think there are a number of avenues that we're pursuing... we're going to leverage these new C&I relationships."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Inquired about expense rationalization. Smith answered, "We had additional incentive compensation and associated taxes in Q4. We also had severance of $4 million in the fourth quarter as well... we continue to work through a number of other cost optimization initiatives."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked scenarios that may require holding excess capital. Otting said, "We've taken on an initiative to move the nonperforming loans out of the bank. And we want to see that, that initiative continues to be successful and we get the nonperforming loans down."
  • Jonathan Rau, Barclays: Asked about new loan yields. Smith said, "C&I loans... are coming on at a spread to SOFR of anywhere from 175 to 300 on a blended basis, you're probably in that 230 basis point range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, pressing for details on payoff activity, funding strategies, and capital deployment, but also acknowledged progress in credit improvement and profitability.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Otting and Smith repeatedly emphasized execution, capital strength, and credit quality improvement, using phrases such as "we're executing on our strategic vision" and "we feel very much on track."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from skepticism about profitability and loan growth to a more constructive, yet probing, stance. Management's confidence was more pronounced due to the return to profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The bank moved from an adjusted net loss of $0.07 per diluted share in Q3 to adjusted net income of $0.06 per diluted share in Q4.
  • Net interest margin improved from 1.91% to 2.14% (including a one-time gain), and CET1 capital ratio increased from 12.45% to 12.83%.
  • C&I loan growth continued, while CRE exposure and brokered deposits were further reduced. Credit quality showed improvement with reductions in nonaccruals and net charge-offs.
  • Management’s tone moved from cautious optimism about returning to profitability to clear affirmation of the turnaround, while analysts’ focus shifted more to sustainable growth and deployment of excess capital.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited higher-than-expected payoff activity as a driver for reduced net interest income guidance.
  • Ongoing focus on CRE exposure and the impact of rent regulation or potential rent freezes in New York City were acknowledged as areas of risk, with Otting stating, "we spend a lot of time looking at various aspects of that portfolio, make sure we understand our risk."
  • The bank is monitoring nonperforming loans and committed to further reductions, with continued scrutiny on loan portfolio quality and expense management.

Final Takeaway

Flagstar Bank’s executive team emphasized that the return to profitability in the fourth quarter was a major milestone, resulting from disciplined execution on strategic initiatives, expense management, and ongoing credit quality improvements. Management outlined clear 2026 and 2027 EPS targets and signaled confidence in continued NIM expansion, C&I loan growth, and a diversified balance sheet, while remaining vigilant about CRE risk and expense optimization as the bank advances its transformation into a top-performing regional institution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ArcBest outlines $150M–$170M capex for 2026 while advancing AI and managed solutions growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ArcBest Corporation (ARCB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Seth Runser highlighted ArcBest's resilience through a prolonged freight recession, stating the company “delivered premium service to our customers, grew daily LTL shipment volumes, restored asset-light profitability, achieved record revenue and shipments in our managed solution and advanced strategic priorities through technology and optimization projects.” Runser emphasized a focus on growth, efficiency, and innovation, citing investments in technology and operational excellence as positioning the company for future success.
  • Runser announced the appointment of Mac Pinkerton as Chief Operating Officer of the asset-light business, noting "Mac brings deep industry expertise and proven leadership. He will help us build on our momentum drive value for our customers and shareholders and further strengthen our competitive position in this important part of our business." The board also welcomed Anne Bordelon and Bobby George as independent directors, bringing added financial and digital transformation expertise.
  • CFO Matt Beasley stated, “Despite continued softness across the freight market, ArcBest delivered solid fourth quarter financial results. Our disciplined approach, operational excellence and strong execution helped us navigate a challenging environment while continuing to create long-term value for our shareholders.”

Outlook

  • Beasley reported that ArcBest expects 2026 net capital expenditures in the range of $150 million to $170 million, reflecting fewer real estate purchases and remodels following several years of targeted investments and optimization projects. He added, "We also anticipate lower spending on revenue equipment in 2026."
  • Management reaffirmed confidence in delivering on the long-term 2028 targets shared at Investor Day and stated, “When we set those targets, we did not anticipate a significant freight market recovery in 2026. Our focus remains on what we can control, driving productivity, maintaining cost discipline and positioning ArcBest for sustainable success regardless of external market conditions.”
  • Guidance for the first quarter includes an expected operating ratio increase of approximately 100 to 200 basis points sequentially for the asset-based segment, which Beasley described as “an improvement relative to typical seasonality due in part to a softer-than-normal fourth quarter though still reflective of the current industry environment.”

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for the fourth quarter was $973 million, down 3% year-over-year. Non-GAAP operating income from continuing operations was $14 million. Adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share were $0.36.
  • Asset-Based segment revenue was $649 million in the quarter. ABF’s operating ratio was 96.2%, a year-over-year increase of 420 basis points. Daily shipments increased by 2% year-over-year and weight per shipment rose slightly, resulting in a nearly 3% increase in tons per day.
  • Asset-Light segment revenue was $354 million, a daily decrease of 5% year-over-year. Shipments per day were up slightly, with growth in Managed Solutions offsetting a reduction in less profitable truckload volumes. Non-GAAP operating results were breakeven for the quarter.
  • The company closed 2025 with $198 million in net capital expenditures, including $25 million in property sales, and returned more than $86 million to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends.

Q&A

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities, Research Division: Questioned January guide and mix changes. Runser responded that heavier dynamic shipments contributed to stronger tonnage and that “our OR normally increases about 260 basis points sequentially from 4Q to 1Q. We’re expecting in that 100 to 200 range... That’s better than history, but also reflective of just the weakness that we see in the macro.”
  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen: Asked about mix normalization. Eddie Sorg explained, “Our business is predominantly manufacturing, industrial production tied to the housing markets. And those 3 verticals have really been impacted... We have a very disciplined pricing approach... but some of it is really just back to the macro.”
  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about January trends and competitive dynamics. CFO Beasley noted some comp specificity to ArcBest but said, "don't really expect any changes on a go-forward basis," and Runser added, “sentiment out there kind of remains cautious.”
  • Reed Seay, Stephens: Asked about asset-light leadership priorities. Mac Pinkerton shared, "I'm more confident after the 3 weeks I've been here will meet our Investor Day targets, and I'm looking forward to making the asset-light business more meaningful in these discussions."
  • Jordan Alliger, Goldman Sachs: Asked about demand signs. Runser pointed to customer focus on cost and efficiency, highlighting double-digit growth in managed solutions and opportunities in discretionary retail, food and beverage, and SMB segments.
  • Chris Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Sought commentary on competitive pricing and margin accretion. Runser stated, "our pricing has continued to strengthen as we've made those adjustments, and that's why you've seen the deferred numbers continue to improve throughout the quarter, and we expect that to continue throughout this year."
  • Additional exchanges covered mix management, AI and productivity initiatives, network capacity, and the long-term EPS target of $12 to $15 by 2028.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone reflected cautious optimism, with repeated questions on mix normalization, competitive dynamics, and margin trajectory. There was persistent probing for signs of industry stabilization and ArcBest’s ability to outperform cyclical weakness.
  • Management maintained a confident yet measured stance, emphasizing what is within their control and reiterating commitments to cost discipline, technology investments, and long-term profitability. During Q&A, responses were detailed and highlighted ongoing initiatives, with Runser and Beasley expressing confidence in meeting strategic targets.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more focused on operational execution and the rollout of technology and AI, while analysts remained attentive to near-term weakness and signs of improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to a more granular focus on cost control, technology-driven efficiency, and capital allocation, with 2026 capex guidance below 5% of revenue.
  • Strategic emphasis increased on AI adoption, managed solutions growth, and operational optimization, with new leadership appointments aimed at strengthening execution.
  • Key metrics showed a sequential decline in revenue and EPS, with asset-light profitability improving to breakeven from a prior-year loss.
  • Management’s confidence in achieving 2028 targets was reiterated, though with acknowledgment of ongoing macro softness. Analysts’ focus remained on margin trajectory, industry mix, and recovery signs, consistent with the previous quarter’s concerns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing freight recession, softness in manufacturing, industrial production, and housing markets as primary challenges.
  • Weather disruptions were acknowledged as recent risks, with Runser noting, “recent severe winter weather...disrupted transportation networks across the industry.”
  • Analysts flagged risks around mix normalization, competitive pricing, and the timing of broader industry recovery.
  • Management’s mitigation efforts include disciplined pricing, cost control, investments in technology, and proactive customer engagement.

Final Takeaway

ArcBest management emphasized the company’s solid operational foundation, disciplined pricing, and ongoing investments in technology and AI-driven efficiency as key levers to navigate a weak freight market. Despite near-term pressures and sequentially lower financial results, ArcBest remains committed to cost discipline, managed solutions growth, and achieving its long-term targets, including $12 to $15 EPS by 2028 and capital expenditures of $150 million to $170 million for 2026, while leveraging new leadership and operational improvements to position for sustainable success.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alpine Banks of Colorado GAAP EPS of $0.07
data.description:
• Alpine Banks of Colorado (ALPIB): Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.07.
  • Total deposits decreased $8.8 million, or 0.1%, to $6.0 billion during fourth quarter 2025 compared to third quarter 2025, primarily due to a $101.7 million decrease in demand deposits and a $90.6 million decrease in certificates of deposit. This decrease was partially offset by a $100.2 million increase in interest-bearing checking accounts and an $84.1 million increase in money fund accounts. Brokered certificates of deposit decreased to $80.0 million on December 31, 2025, compared to $160.0 million on September 30, 2025.

data.title: Rithm Acquisition receives NYSE non-compliance notice
data.description:
• Rithm Acquisition (RAC) received a notice from the NYSE on January 26, 2026, stating it is not in compliance with the requirement to maintain at least 300 public stockholders.
  • The company has 45 days from receipt of the notice to submit a plan to regain compliance, with an initial deadline of March 12, 2026.
  • RAC plans to promptly submit a business plan outlining how it expects to return to compliance within 18 months of receiving the notice.

data.title: Federated Hermes plans Hong Kong expansion and projects $2.7B in unfunded institutional mandates for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Federated Hermes, Inc. (FHI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Donahue, President, CEO & Chairman, highlighted a record assets under management figure of $903 billion at year-end, led by gains in money market and equity strategies. He emphasized, "2025 saw record gross equity sales of $31 billion, including $9 billion in the fourth quarter. Fourth quarter net equity sales were $1.5 billion. Our full year 2025 net equity sales of $4.6 billion showed substantial improvement from net redemptions of $10.7 billion in 2004."
  • Donahue noted strong performance in MDT fundamental quant strategies, stating, "MDT equity and market neutral strategies had a record $4 billion of gross sales and over $2 billion in net sales in the fourth quarter. For 2025, MDT gross sales of $19.1 billion, and net sales of $13 billion were both record highs."
  • He reported, "We are progressing towards the FCP acquisition -- to closing the FCP acquisition during the first half of '26. The acquisition will add U.S. multifamily housing expertise to our long-standing U.K.-based real estate capabilities."
  • Donahue announced, "We announced plans to open a Hong Kong office to capitalize on the region's rapidly growing wealth market. Subject to regulatory and other necessary approvals, the planned Hong Kong office represents a strategic expansion as we deepen relationships across the Asia Pacific region."
  • Thomas Donahue, VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director, stated, "For Q4 compared to the prior quarter, total revenue increased $13.4 million, or 3%. Revenue from higher money market assets provided $8 million of this increase, while higher equity assets added $5.5 million. Q4 revenue included $8.2 million of real estate development fees for projects that did not advance into construction."

Outlook

  • Donahue outlined, "Across our long-term investment platform, we began 2026 with about $2.7 billion in net institutional mandates yet to fund into both funds and separate accounts. Approximately $1.2 billion on a net basis is expected to come into private market strategies, including direct lending, private equity and trade finance. Equities expected additions totaled $1.4 billion, with about $1.3 billion into MDT strategies, and $100 million into international and global equity strategies. Fixed income is expected to have net sales of about $100 million into a low-duration strategy."
  • The company expects to close the FCP acquisition during the first half of 2026, using $215.8 million in cash and $23.2 million in FHI Class B stock for the initial purchase price.
  • Looking ahead to Q1, the CFO stated, "The impact of fewer days is expected to result in about $10.2 million in lower revenues and about $2.6 million in lower distribution expenses. In addition, compensation and related expense is expected to be higher than Q4, primarily due to about $8 million of seasonally higher expenses for stock compensation and payroll taxes."

Financial Results

  • Total Q4 carried interest and performance fees were $1.6 million, compared to $3.6 million in the prior quarter.
  • Q4 operating expenses increased by $7.3 million, or 2%, from the prior quarter due mainly to higher distribution expense of $8.8 million from higher fund assets.
  • Transaction costs from the FCP acquisition were about $1.3 million in Q4, with additional transaction costs in 2026 estimated to be approximately $9.2 million.
  • In the other expense line item, FX and related expense decreased by $3.1 million in Q4 compared to the prior quarter.
  • The effective tax rate was 24.4% for Q4, with expectations for the rate to be in the 25% to 28% range for 2026.
  • At the end of 2025, cash and investments were $724 million; excluding noncontrolling interests, $680 million.

Q&A

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: Questioned the increase in distribution costs relative to money market fund growth. Raymond Hanley, President, responded, "A significant amount of assets came into share class where there are higher than average distribution expenses... those kind of changes in mix can impact that."
  • Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about the transition of portfolio managers retiring. Donahue replied, "The succession planning ... has gone on for many, many years. And in every one of those cases... we don't look for any disruption in the investment management techniques performance."
  • William Katz, TD Cowen, Research Division: Inquired about tokenization demand. Donahue explained, "On the end demand from clients, it's not as robust as what you might expect from all the press... It's getting ready for tomorrow."
  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research US LP: Asked about Fed rate cuts and institutional rotation into money funds. Deborah Cunningham, Executive VP & Chief Investment Officer of Money Markets, shared, "Our outlook... for 2026 is one rate cut by 25 basis points... But in either case, you're looking at an end terminal rate that is north of 3%..."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost trends, succession planning, and tokenization, asking detailed questions about mix and future demand, reflecting a neutral-to-slightly-positive tone as they probed for underlying drivers and sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and generally positive tone, emphasizing the strength of succession planning, robust product demand, and strategic digital initiatives. Donahue stated, "We believe these efforts position the firm well for the digital transition."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains measured and focused on growth drivers, while management continues to project confidence but increased specificity on digital and global expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for unfunded institutional mandates increased from $2.1 billion at the start of Q4 to $2.7 billion at the start of 2026, with a larger proportion allocated to MDT strategies and private markets.
  • The company provided more explicit discussion of digital asset initiatives, including specific collaborations and the planned opening of a Hong Kong office, compared to prior quarter’s general references to digital and international expansion.
  • Management’s tone remains positive but more focused on execution and strategic platform building, especially in digital assets and Asia-Pacific.
  • Analysts' questions shifted to focus more on distribution costs and the sustainability of strong product flows, while maintaining interest in digital and global growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted fixed income outflows due to large public entities’ movements but described these as routine.
  • Succession planning for retiring portfolio managers was highlighted, with reassurance that replacements have significant tenure and experience.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the rapid increase in distribution costs, particularly in money market share classes with higher expenses.
  • Tokenization efforts were described as early-stage, with actual client demand still limited and dependent on future regulatory and market developments.

Final Takeaway

Federated Hermes ended 2025 with record assets under management and set the stage for further global growth with a planned Hong Kong office and continued digital asset initiatives. The company’s robust pipeline of $2.7 billion in unfunded institutional mandates and strong demand for MDT and private market strategies support management’s optimistic outlook. While challenges include succession transitions, distribution cost pressures, and evolving digital asset demand, management emphasized strategic readiness and confidence in execution for 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Merion Road Capital Management adds JHG and ACNT in Q4
data.description:
Merion Road Capital Management, in its fourth-quarter client letter, said that it acquired shares of Janus Henderson Group (JHG)  after the group received a non-binding acquisition proposal by their largest shareholder to acquire the company at $46.00. 

Additionally, the fund built a position in Ascent Industries (ACNT), a small specialty chemicals company that is transforming into a pure play with a pristine balance.

The fund said that its cash position sits at 16% of the portfolio, while a relatively safe merger arbitrage position accounts for another 6%.

Source: client letter
